prompt: Analyze the complex issue of liability allocation in accidents involving vehicles with advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) operating in a shared human-machine driving context. Your analysis should integrate technical principles of ADAS, existing legal frameworks, and relevant case law to systematically examine the boundaries of responsibility between the driver and the system. Conclude with proposed regulatory guidelines or recommendations.
article:
## 1) Framing the problem: “shared human–machine driving” and why liability is hard

### 1.1 The core technical/legal mismatch
Most real-world “ADAS crashes” arise with **SAE Level 1–2 driver support** systems (ACC, lane centering/LKA, AEB, traffic jam assist), where **vehicle motion control may be automated but responsibility for supervision and hazard response largely remains human**.

SAE J3016 is explicit that it is **descriptive (not legal)** and that levels are defined by how the **dynamic driving task (DDT)** and **DDT fallback** are allocated, not by marketing labels or perceived capability [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf). For:
- **Level 1**: automation performs *either* sustained lateral *or* sustained longitudinal control; the **driver performs the rest of the DDT**, supervises, and immediately performs the full DDT when needed [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf).  
- **Level 2**: automation performs **both sustained lateral and longitudinal control**, but the **driver is still expected to perform OEDR (object and event detection and response)**, supervise the system, and immediately perform the entire DDT whenever required [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf).  

A key practical complication is that the market often uses terms like **“Level 2+”**, which SAE J3016 commentary warns is not a recognized SAE category (and “+” terminology is prohibited in the J3016 guidance) [[3]](https://users.ece.cmu.edu/~koopman/j3016/). This increases the risk that consumers, jurors, and even regulators interpret capability as higher than it is.

### 1.2 Why liability allocation is intrinsically coupled to human factors
ISO 21448 (SOTIF) is central for understanding *why crashes happen even without component “failure”*: hazards can arise from **functional insufficiencies** such as perception limits, robustness gaps, scenario coverage gaps, and **divergent human expectations / HMI design** [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf). SOTIF explicitly includes:
- hazards from **inability to perceive the environment correctly**, lack of robustness to environmental conditions, and unexpected algorithm behavior [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf)
- hazards caused by **mode confusion** (driver thinks active when not, or vice versa) and **lack of driver attention while using Level 2** [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf)
- **reasonably foreseeable misuse** as within scope [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf)

This matters legally because it aligns directly with doctrines like **foreseeability**, **failure to warn**, and **design defect** in product liability, and with negligence concepts like **reasonable use**, **reasonable reliance**, and **comparative fault**.

---

## 2) Technical principles that define the boundary of responsibility

### 2.1 The “DDT split” is the legal fault line (even when users don’t perceive it)
For Level 2 systems, the machine may steer/brake/accelerate, but (per SAE) the **driver still must monitor the roadway and intervene** [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf). This allocation is the default baseline most courts and regulators start from when assigning human responsibility—unless the plaintiff proves a product defect, inadequate warnings, misleading marketing, or a regulatory scheme shifting responsibility.

### 2.2 Operational Design Domain (ODD): the hidden “terms of performance”
Although consumers often experience Level 2 ADAS as generally usable, SAE frames these systems as operating within a **limited ODD** [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf). ISO 21448 highlights that **ODD exits** (e.g., visibility degradation; sensor occlusion) are a classic trigger for hazardous behavior, even absent malfunction [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf).

Practically, ODD boundaries are often *not enforced* by geofencing and are communicated through manuals/HMI rather than hard constraints—so the boundary between “driver misuse” and “foreseeable misuse induced by permissive design” is frequently contested (see Section 4).

### 2.3 Perception and classification limits (false negatives and false positives)
**False negatives**: failure to detect a hazard (stopped vehicle, cross-traffic, pedestrian) in time can result in no braking or inadequate warning—often explained as SOTIF insufficiency (scenario coverage/perception) [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf).

**False positives**: unintended braking (“phantom braking”) can itself create danger (rear-end risk). NHTSA explicitly treats unintended braking as a safety risk and retained a false-positive AEB test (steel trench plate scenario) because unnecessary hard braking can cause rear-end crashes, particularly at speed [[13]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2024-11/NCAP-Final-Decision-Notice-Advanced-Driver-Assistance-Systems-Roadmap-11182024-web.pdf). This is important for liability: a “safety feature” that unpredictably brakes may be argued as **defectively designed** or **unreasonably dangerous**, not merely a driver problem.

### 2.4 Driver monitoring and takeover alerts: engineering controls that map onto legal “reasonableness”
UNECE regulatory development texts (especially steering automation under R79 proposals) show an emerging regulatory theory: because Level 2 relies on supervision, systems should include **driver availability recognition**, escalating warnings, and **transition demands**, and if ignored, a **minimum risk manoeuvre** concept appears (more prominent in Level 3 ALKS frameworks, but increasingly influential as an expectation) [[5]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/29884732/ACSF-06-13%20%28J%29%20Proposal%20to%20amend%20R79%20based%20on%20ACSF-05-16.pdf).  

Even where not yet binding for all Level 2 implementations, these texts matter in court as evidence of:
- **state of the art**
- what risks are **known and addressable**
- what “reasonable” design and warning strategies look like

ISO 15005’s HMI principles reinforce that interface timing, consistency, self-descriptiveness, and workload management are safety-critical and affect driver performance under stress—relevant to whether warnings are adequate and whether mode confusion is preventable [[15]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/69238/02ee6a4ef27f451f83c8b338c0dac275/ISO-15005-2017.pdf).

### 2.5 Empirical evidence: partial automation changes driver behavior (predictably)
Naturalistic and survey research supports a causal chain that courts increasingly recognize: **partial automation encourages off-road glances and secondary task engagement**, degrading OEDR and reaction time.

- A naturalistic study found drivers looked away from the road more when automation was active and were more likely to browse on phones during automated driving segments [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33385962/).  
- A survey study of Level 2 users reported increased secondary task engagement and documented known misuse patterns: prolonged hands-free use, ignoring warnings, testing limits, mode confusion, and use in bad weather [[21]](https://escholarship.org/content/qt4j17h6xw/qt4j17h6xw.pdf).  
- Meta-analysis of takeover research (mainly Level 3 but relevant to sudden disengagement events) shows non-driving tasks degrade takeover performance, especially with overlapping cognitive demands [[22]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33307821/).  

These findings are crucial for liability allocation because they support arguments that certain “misuse” is **foreseeable**, and therefore must be mitigated by design (driver monitoring, ODD enforcement, safer fallback) rather than simply blamed on the user—consistent with ISO 21448’s inclusion of foreseeable misuse and HMI risks [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf).

---

## 3) A systematic accident-causation & responsibility model for shared-control ADAS

A practical way to allocate liability is to classify crashes into **interaction archetypes**, each with a typical evidentiary footprint and a recurring legal theory set.

### Archetype A — “Classic driver negligence with ADAS merely present”
**Pattern**
- ADAS engaged or available, but crash caused by clear human error (speeding, distraction, impairment), and the system either was not designed to handle the scenario or was overridden.

**Evidence**
- EDR speed/brake/accelerator traces (US 49 CFR Part 563 baseline elements include speed, throttle, brake status) [[95]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/CFR-2011-title49-vol6/pdf/CFR-2011-title49-vol6-part563.pdf)
- ADAS engagement logs (often proprietary; may be partially captured in OEM telemetry)
- driver admissions, phone data

**Liability tendency**
- Primary driver fault under negligence.
- OEM liability less likely unless warnings/marketing created unreasonable expectations, or system did something affirmatively dangerous.

**Illustration**
NTSB’s Spring, Texas 2021 crash (Tesla Model S) ultimately attributed probable cause to **excessive speed and impairment**; NTSB testing also found Autosteer not available on that road segment, illustrating that “ADAS involvement” can be over-assumed by the public [[132]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/Pages/HWY21FH007.aspx), [[128]](https://data.ntsb.gov/Docket/Document/docBLOB).

### Archetype B — “Foreseeable overreliance + weak monitoring/controls (system invites misuse)”
**Pattern**
- Driver disengages (hands off, eyes off, distraction) while Level 2 controls speed/lane.
- Crash occurs in a situation where a reasonably attentive driver would have avoided it.

**Key technical question**
Did the system’s **driver monitoring / engagement enforcement** and HMI controls reasonably mitigate predictable disengagement?

**Evidence**
- Driver engagement warnings, torque/attention monitoring status
- design documents/manuals; recall/ODI findings
- crash similarity evidence for notice

**Regulatory anchor**
NHTSA’s EA22-002 (Tesla Autopilot) found a mismatch: **high control authority + weak usage controls**, leading to foreseeable misuse and avoidable crashes; it identified crash trend categories including frontal impacts with adequate time for attentive driver response and roadway departures tied to inadvertent disengagement and low-traction operation, and concluded Autopilot controls were insufficient to ensure attention/appropriate use [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf). Tesla’s recall 23V-838 explicitly framed the defect as insufficient prominence/scope of controls to prevent misuse [[122]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCLRPT-23V838-8276.PDF), with ODI opening a recall query to test remedy adequacy [[126]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2024/INOA-RQ24009-12046.pdf).

**Liability tendency**
- Shared fault: driver comparative negligence + OEM design defect/failure-to-warn, especially if evidence shows the system predictably permits disengagement.
- Punitive exposure can arise if marketing/control choices are framed as reckless.

**Illustration (NTSB Williston 2016)**
NTSB found probable cause included driver inattention due to overreliance, and importantly stated that **operational design permitted prolonged disengagement and use inconsistent with guidance/warnings**, contributing to overreliance [[92]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/accidentreports/reports/har1702.pdf).

### Archetype C — “Mode confusion / ambiguous status / misleading cues”
**Pattern**
- Driver believes automation is active (or will brake), but it is canceled, limited, or overridden; or driver does not understand the consequences of override.

**Evidence**
- HMI state transitions, alerts, whether the driver received/acknowledged warnings
- manufacturer design choices that allow operation outside recommended ODD
- training prompts, “nag” design, escalation logic

**Technical/legal bridge**
ISO 21448 treats mode confusion and divergent expectations as direct hazard sources and places them within intended functionality safety analysis [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf). UNECE proposals emphasize clear mode status, escalating transition warnings, and driver availability recognition [[5]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/29884732/ACSF-06-13%20%28J%29%20Proposal%20to%20amend%20R79%20based%20on%20ACSF-05-16.pdf).

**Illustration (Florida Keys crash litigation)**
In *Benavides v. Tesla* (S.D. Fla.), the court record describes a dispute about what remained active when the driver used the accelerator to override speed restriction, and whether warnings adequately conveyed that TACC would not brake; the narrative includes that the vehicle detected roadway elements but did not alert or brake, while driver looked down for his phone [[75]](https://cases.justia.com/federal/district-courts/florida/flsdce/1:2021cv21940/593426/428/0.pdf). The evidentiary rulings later treated distracted-driving-with-Autopilot as central to notice and foreseeability, not as a complete dissimilarity that breaks causation [[76]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940/pdf/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940-6.pdf).

### Archetype D — “Functional insufficiency (SOTIF) in edge cases inside perceived ODD”
**Pattern**
- Driver uses system in a context that seems normal (e.g., daylight highway), but perception/control fails due to glare, faded markings, atypical object, sensor occlusion, or rare scenario.

**Evidence**
- sensor conditions (glare, fog), camera/radar confidence (if logged)
- OEM ODD definition vs actual system behavior
- post-market incident trend data

**Illustration (reduced visibility)**
NHTSA ODI opened PE24031 to evaluate Tesla FSD-Supervised crashes in **reduced visibility** (sun glare, fog, airborne dust), including a fatal pedestrian strike, focusing on whether controls detect/respond appropriately to such conditions and whether updates affected performance [[11]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2024/INOA-PE24031-23232.pdf). This is a classic “SOTIF + ODD ambiguity” zone.

### Archetype E — “System introduces hazard (e.g., phantom braking)”
**Pattern**
- ADAS behavior itself creates risk; driver intervention may avoid crash, but the system’s unexpected action is the initiating hazard.

**Evidence**
- deceleration trace, object classification, warning timing
- system design expectations; consumer expectation arguments

**Regulatory anchor**
NHTSA’s NCAP decision documents emphasize unintended braking is a safety problem that can cause rear-end crashes, supporting defect arguments where phantom braking is frequent and severe [[13]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2024-11/NCAP-Final-Decision-Notice-Advanced-Driver-Assistance-Systems-Roadmap-11182024-web.pdf).

**Illustration (Germany phantom braking ruling as reported)**
A German Regional Court (Traunstein) ruling (as reported/quoted in secondary sources) treated unprovoked braking as a **defect** impairing normal usability and potentially creating critical traffic situations, stating it does not matter that the driver can intervene because the need for intervention without discernible external influence is itself abnormal at that price point [[89]](https://ru.law/en/erstes-urteil-zu-den-phantom-bremsungen-tesla-zur-nachbesserung-verurteilt/), [[88]](https://www.carscoops.com/2025/02/german-court-finds-teslas-autopilot-defective-after-lawsuit/). (The holding should be verified against the official judgment text, but it signals how consumer-expectation reasoning can attach to ADAS behavior.)

---

## 4) Legal frameworks governing liability allocation (with emphasis on Level 2 shared-control)

### 4.1 United States: state tort + product liability, shaped by federal regulation and NHTSA enforcement

#### (a) Negligence and comparative fault
Most states allocate accident liability using negligence and some form of comparative fault. In Level 2 cases, defendants typically argue:
- driver breached duty by distraction, speeding, impairment, hands-off operation
- system was “assist only” and warnings placed responsibility on the driver

Plaintiffs counter with:
- “reasonable reliance” shaped by system design, marketing, and prior performance
- foreseeable misuse that should have been designed against (DMS, ODD constraints)

#### (b) Product liability theories: design defect, failure to warn, misrepresentation
Common theories in ADAS crashes:
- **Design defect**: inadequate driver engagement enforcement, permissive use outside safe ODD, unsafe override logic, inadequate fallback behavior (even within Level 2), unreasonable hazard creation (phantom braking).
- **Failure to warn**: warnings not salient, not timely, buried in manuals, inconsistent with marketing, or not specific to known failure modes.
- **Misrepresentation / consumer protection**: marketing claims cause overreliance.

Restatement-oriented commentary emphasizes modern product liability’s focus on design rather than relying on warnings, and the role of “obvious danger” concepts—highly relevant where manufacturers argue “everyone knows you must pay attention,” while plaintiffs argue the *product design* encourages otherwise [[30]](https://www.floridabar.org/the-florida-bar-journal/the-restatement-third-of-torts-products-liability-the-tension-between-product-design-and-product-warnings/).

#### (c) Federal regulation’s role: not a direct liability code, but powerful evidence
NHTSA’s Standing General Order (SGO) defines Level 2 as requiring the driver remain fully engaged and sets crash reporting requirements when Level 2 was engaged within 30 seconds and certain severity thresholds apply [[8]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/laws-regulations/standing-general-order-crash-reporting), [[9]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2022-06/ADAS-L2-SGO-Report-June-2022.pdf). While not allocating tort liability, the SGO creates:
- a stream of reported incidents
- a regulatory record that can show notice, trend awareness, and defect investigations

NHTSA defect investigations and recalls are particularly influential. For Tesla Autosteer/Autopilot:
- Recall 23V-838 framed the defect as insufficient controls to prevent misuse (2+ million vehicles) [[121]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCAK-23V838-3395.pdf), [[122]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCLRPT-23V838-8276.PDF).
- ODI’s EA22-002 closing resume concluded weak engagement controls were not appropriate for permissive capabilities, creating a critical safety gap and foreseeable misuse [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf).
- ODI opened RQ24009 to evaluate whether the recall remedy is adequate, citing post-remedy crash events and preliminary NHTSA testing concerns, including that parts of the remedy may be opt-in/reversible [[126]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2024/INOA-RQ24009-12046.pdf).

Separately, U.S. EDR regulation (49 CFR Part 563) standardizes certain crash-relevant data elements (speed, brake, throttle, delta-V), improving baseline reconstruction evidence but generally not guaranteeing ADAS state variables [[95]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/CFR-2011-title49-vol6/pdf/CFR-2011-title49-vol6-part563.pdf). NHTSA has expanded pre-crash capture in later rulemaking (20s at higher sample rate for certain elements), improving the ability to reconstruct lead-up behavior and driver inputs [[97]](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/12/18/2024-29862/event-data-recorders).

**Driver Privacy Act** concepts (as reflected in bill text) treat EDR data as owned by the vehicle owner/lessee, affecting access pathways and requiring exceptions or legal process for third parties [[107]](https://www.congress.gov/bill/114th-congress/senate-bill/766/text).

#### (d) Preemption and compliance defenses (limits)
Product liability defenses often cite compliance with FMVSS and other standards. Secondary comparative materials note that federal preemption can arise where state claims conflict with federal requirements, but also that many states do not treat regulatory compliance as a full defense [[29]](https://iclg.com/practice-areas/product-liability-laws-and-regulations/usa). In ADAS, this is complicated because:
- many Level 2 behaviors historically were not governed by prescriptive FMVSS performance standards
- defects may arise from software/HMI choices beyond explicit regulatory minimums

### 4.2 European Union: strict product liability modernization + type-approval safety duties + data governance

#### (a) Revised Product Liability Directive (EU) 2024/2853 (PLD)
The revised PLD is widely described as modernizing strict liability to better cover **software and AI**, expanding liable economic operators, and easing claimants’ evidentiary burden through disclosure mechanisms and presumptions [[40]](https://www.hoganlovells.com/en/publications/eu-introduces-comprehensive-digitalera-product-liability-directive), [[44]](https://cms.law/en/fra/news-information/new-evidence-law-in-product-liability), [[45]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-E2LDPXX-2024). Recitals in the Directive discuss the “development risk” defense (state of scientific/technical knowledge) while acknowledging debates about consumer protection limits [[39]](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/).

Secondary analyses emphasize features particularly relevant to shared-control ADAS:
- software/updates as “product” elements and liability for defects tied to updates or missing necessary updates [[37]](https://www.osborneclarke.com/insights/how-do-eu-product-liability-regulations-support-innovation-and-what-do-protect-against), [[45]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-E2LDPXX-2024)
- disclosure of evidence and burden-shifting presumptions where defendants fail to disclose [[44]](https://cms.law/en/fra/news-information/new-evidence-law-in-product-liability)
- supply-chain liability hierarchy to ensure an EU-based responsible party exists [[43]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2025/eu-revised-product-liability-directive-2024-navigating-the-new-liability-framework-for-noneu-manufac)

This is crucial in ADAS because the most probative evidence often sits with the OEM (proprietary logs, training data, post-market analytics). A disclosure regime can materially change practical liability allocation by reducing information asymmetry.

#### (b) General Safety Regulation (EU) 2019/2144 and EDR requirements
EU law mandates EDR fitment and detailed type-approval requirements through delegated acts; an EDR requirements summary document describes non-deactivation, accuracy, survivability, and that recorded data can be made available to national authorities for accident research and analysis via standardized interfaces under GDPR constraints [[46]](https://circabc.europa.eu/sd/a/3bee4345-fd03-4681-8e9b-8a32e14196c8/Event%20Data%20Recorder%20rev.pdf). Delegated Regulation (EU) 2022/545 supplements these obligations (light vehicles) [[47]](https://lawthek.eu/detail/31ae1bb1-e083-400d-bcf4-052ec9fdd989/en/SINGLE).

While these frameworks are not primarily designed to adjudicate private liability, they shape:
- baseline evidence availability
- what “reasonable safety design” means in the EU market

#### (c) EU Data Act (Regulation (EU) 2023/2854) and GDPR interaction
The Data Act creates user-centric rights to access and share connected-product data, including vehicles, with third parties (e.g., insurers), and requires data be made accessible without undue delay in usable formats in certain circumstances (per secondary summaries) [[51]](https://www.ey.com/en_gr/technical/tax/tax-alerts/regulation-eu-2023-2854-on-harmonised-rules-on-fair-access-to-and-use-of-data), [[50]](https://www.fiatprofessional.com/eu-data-ac). However, the Data Act itself underscores it **does not create a legal basis for personal data processing** where the user is not the data subject; GDPR Article 6 legal basis is still required [[39]](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/).  

This matters for liability because insurers and plaintiffs may need ADAS logs, but privacy law can constrain access unless lawful basis, minimization, and purpose limitation are satisfied.

### 4.3 United Kingdom: sharp statutory distinction between “driver-assistance” and “self-driving”

The UK has unusually direct statutes for automated vehicles, which clarifies liability boundaries—but largely **only once a vehicle is legally classified as “driving itself.”**

#### (a) Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 (AEVA)
For accidents caused by an automated vehicle “when driving itself,” AEVA places primary compensation liability on the **insurer (or owner if uninsured)**, with **contributory negligence** reductions and explicit defenses for inappropriate activation and software update failures [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18). It also grants insurers/owners rights of action to recover against responsible parties (subrogation-like) [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18). Critically:
- Insurer/owner not liable to the person in charge if the accident was wholly due to negligence in allowing the vehicle to begin driving itself when it was not appropriate [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18).
- Insurer liability can be limited/excluded for prohibited software alterations or failure to install safety-critical updates known/should be known [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18).

#### (b) Automated Vehicles Act 2024 (AVA)
AVA 2024 builds a full authorization regime and (per secondary briefings) provides that when an authorized self-driving feature is engaged in a “user-in-charge” journey, the user is generally immune from “manner of driving” offenses while the system is driving, shifting responsibility toward the authorized self-driving entity (ASDE), subject to exceptions (e.g., after transition demand period ends) [[59]](https://www.hfw.com/app/uploads/2024/07/006179-HFW-UKs-Automated-Vehicles-Act-2024.pdf). Importantly, the same briefings emphasize that **driver-assistance features (AEB, active cruise, lane keeping assist)** remain in the conventional regime—i.e., drivers remain responsible when using those [[59]](https://www.hfw.com/app/uploads/2024/07/006179-HFW-UKs-Automated-Vehicles-Act-2024.pdf).  

This provides a policy model: **legal responsibility shifts only when the system meets a regulated “self-driving” threshold**; below that (Level 2), responsibility largely stays with the driver unless product liability is proven.

---

## 5) Case law and enforcement signals: how responsibility boundaries are being drawn

### 5.1 U.S. litigation: patterns and what courts/juries are doing

#### (a) *Benavides v. Tesla* (Florida Keys crash): shared responsibility is now jury-plausible
In *Benavides v. Tesla* (S.D. Fla.), the record describes a driver using Autopilot/TACC elements, overriding speed restriction with accelerator, looking down for a phone, and the vehicle not issuing audio warning or braking despite detecting roadway features; disputes existed about which functions were active and whether override disabled braking features [[75]](https://cases.justia.com/federal/district-courts/florida/flsdce/1:2021cv21940/593426/428/0.pdf).  

The court’s later evidentiary ruling allowed certain “substantially similar” crashes for notice purposes focusing on the similarity that drivers were distracted despite Tesla’s monitoring/warning approach, and rejected the idea that driver distraction makes prior events irrelevant; it treated distracted-use as central to foreseeability and notice [[76]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940/pdf/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940-6.pdf).  

A February 2026 report describes a Florida jury verdict allocating Tesla partial liability with substantial punitive damages in this crash (as reported, Tesla responsible for one-third compensatory plus punitive) [[77]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c93dqpkwx4xo). Even allowing for post-trial motions/appeals, this is an important signal: **driver error does not automatically preclude OEM liability** when plaintiffs frame weak controls, permissive ODD use, and expectation-setting.

#### (b) *Wang v. Tesla* (E.D.N.Y.): marketing claims require specific reliance proof
In *Wang v. Tesla*, plaintiffs alleged Autopilot failed to react and AEB failed in dense traffic; the court dismissed fraud claims for failure to plead with particularity and to show reliance on specific pre-purchase statements, and found insufficient facts to infer fraudulent intent from a salesman’s “close your eyes and relax” statement alone [[78]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCOURTS-nyed-1_20-cv-03040/pdf/USCOURTS-nyed-1_20-cv-03040-1.pdf).  

This shows a key boundary: **misrepresentation theories can shift responsibility toward the OEM, but only with strong, specific evidence of exposure, reliance, and intent**, not generalized “Autopilot hype” allegations.

#### (c) *Berkoski v. Honda* (N.J. App. Div. 2025): “failure to equip with ADAS” is not easily a defect
New Jersey’s published opinion rejected a theory that a vehicle was defectively designed because it lacked LDW/LKA, emphasizing the car had a functioning steering system and was safe and fit for driving; the absence of the latest driver-assistance tech was not a design defect as a matter of law under the NJ Products Liability Act on these facts [[86]](https://www.njcourts.gov/system/files/court-opinions/2025/a2887-22.pdf).  

Implication: plaintiffs generally do better attacking **defective implementation** of installed ADAS (monitoring, warnings, unsafe behaviors) than claiming liability for not installing ADAS.

### 5.2 NTSB findings: not “liability,” but highly influential on foreseeability and design duties
NTSB reports cannot be used to assign civil liability (statutory constraints are often noted in NTSB reports and reflected in court evidentiary rulings) [[130]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/AccidentReports/Reports/HAR2001.pdf), and courts may bar NTSB Board reports/recommendations in jury trials while allowing factual investigator materials [[76]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940/pdf/USCOURTS-flsd-1_21-cv-21940-6.pdf). Still, NTSB’s design/behavior findings shape:
- what risks are known
- what mitigations are feasible
- the plausibility of foreseeable misuse

Key NTSB findings:
- **Williston 2016 (HAR-17-02)**: driver failed to yield and driver inattention due to overreliance; crucially, NTSB found contributing factor was operational design that permitted prolonged disengagement and use inconsistent with guidance/warnings [[92]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/accidentreports/reports/har1702.pdf).  
- **Mountain View 2018 (HAR-20-01)**: probable cause included Autopilot steering into gore due to system limitations and driver’s lack of response due to distraction and overreliance; contributing factor: ineffective driver engagement monitoring facilitating complacency/inattentiveness [[129]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/Pages/HWY18FH011.aspx), [[130]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/AccidentReports/Reports/HAR2001.pdf).  
- **Delray Beach 2019 (HAB-20-01)**: Autopilot engaged; driver took no evasive action; NTSB collected extensive video/log data including proprietary “Carlog” and camera clips, illustrating that liability disputes often hinge on OEM-controlled telemetry rather than only generic EDR [[131]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/AccidentReports/Reports/HAB2001.pdf).

### 5.3 Regulatory enforcement: NHTSA recall and ODI reasoning directly target “misuse prevention” as a safety obligation
ODI’s EA22-002 closing document (Tesla Autopilot) is unusually direct in human factors terms:
- Autopilot “invited greater driver confidence” due to ease of engagement and control authority
- weak engagement controls were not appropriate, creating a safety gap between expectations and true capabilities
- that gap led to foreseeable misuse and avoidable crashes [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf)

Tesla’s recall (23V-838) characterizes the defect in similar terms: “prominence and scope of the feature’s controls may not be sufficient to prevent driver misuse” [[122]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCLRPT-23V838-8276.PDF). ODI then opened RQ24009 questioning remedy adequacy, citing post-remedy crashes and NHTSA tests, and noting opt-in/reversibility concerns [[126]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2024/INOA-RQ24009-12046.pdf).

These records can function in civil litigation as:
- notice evidence
- state-of-knowledge evidence
- support for negligence/design defect theories
- rebuttal to “the driver alone caused it” narratives where design is alleged to facilitate misuse

### 5.4 Marketing enforcement: boundary policing to prevent “self-driving” misconceptions
California DMV administrative documents (as hosted in secondary repositories) alleged Tesla’s “Autopilot” and “Full Self-Driving Capability” marketing was misleading and tantamount to autonomy claims, with disclaimers not curing misleading impressions [[133]](https://www.thedrive.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/08/21-02189-California-DMV-Tesla-Autopilot-Complaint.pdf). A reported DMV news alert (again hosted secondarily) states that in December 2025 DMV adopted an ALJ decision finding Tesla violated state law by misleadingly using “autopilot” and “Full Self-Driving Capability,” modifying penalties and requiring corrective action within a time window to avoid license suspension [[135]](https://dpadocs.dpaq.de/21831_12_16_25_Tesla_Administrative_Decision_Released_by_DMV.pdf). (These should be corroborated with official DMV/OAH sources for litigation use, but they reflect a strong regulatory stance: **naming and marketing can shift the responsibility boundary by shaping consumer expectations**.)

Internationally, a German court (Munich) reportedly banned Tesla from using certain autonomy-leaning advertising phrases, finding consumers misled about automated driving abilities [[87]](https://www.cnbc.com/2020/07/14/tesla-autopilot-self-driving-false-advertising-germany.html). This is aligned with the broader regulatory theory: preventing “capability inflation” reduces foreseeable misuse.

---

## 6) Evidence and data: the practical hinge of fault allocation

### 6.1 Baseline EDR is necessary but often insufficient for ADAS disputes
U.S. Part 563 EDR data includes speed, throttle, brake status, delta-V, etc., and is essential for reconstructing whether the driver braked/steered and when [[95]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/CFR-2011-title49-vol6/pdf/CFR-2011-title49-vol6-part563.pdf). EU EDR frameworks emphasize survivability, non-deactivation, and standardized retrieval for authorities under privacy constraints [[46]](https://circabc.europa.eu/sd/a/3bee4345-fd03-4681-8e9b-8a32e14196c8/Event%20Data%20Recorder%20rev.pdf).  

But ADAS liability disputes often require **ADAS-specific variables**:
- automation engagement state and mode transitions
- warning timing and type (visual/haptic/auditory)
- driver monitoring results (hands/eyes presence)
- perception confidence / detected object types (in advanced logs)
- whether the system was within its declared ODD

NHTSA itself notes that traditional EDR rules historically did not require active safety feature details, limiting research and reconstruction [[97]](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/12/18/2024-29862/event-data-recorders).

### 6.2 Proprietary logs create information asymmetry (and therefore liability asymmetry)
Research on EDR for automated vehicles highlights that OEMs often store richer proprietary ADAS data (sometimes including video/object data), but triggering and access can be opaque; manufacturers may control what is disclosed to independent experts and courts, risking “data chaos” and mistrust if datasets conflict or appear selective [[111]](https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/eng-2020-0007/pdf).  

This is exactly where modern EU PLD disclosure/burden-shifting provisions (as summarized) become legally potent by reducing asymmetry [[44]](https://cms.law/en/fra/news-information/new-evidence-law-in-product-liability), and where U.S. discovery/spoliation doctrine can become outcome-determinative.

### 6.3 Preservation/spoliation: ADAS data is perishable
Electronic evidence principles (Sedona) emphasize reasonable, proportional preservation duties and sanctions for intentional deprivation of relevant ESI [[112]](https://www.thesedonaconference.org/file-download/download/public/5140). In vehicle cases, event recorder and telematics data can overwrite quickly; failure to preserve can lead to adverse inferences or sanctions under general spoliation doctrines [[113]](https://www.luederlaw.com/evidence-preservation-event-data-recorders-edr-and-spoliation-issues/), [[112]](https://www.thesedonaconference.org/file-download/download/public/5140).  

Because ADAS liability hinges on “what mode was active, what warnings occurred,” early preservation letters and neutral imaging protocols are critical.

### 6.4 Privacy and access: EU Data Act helps, but GDPR still controls personal data
The EU Data Act is expected to empower users to access/share connected vehicle data with third parties like insurers in standard formats [[50]](https://www.fiatprofessional.com/eu-data-ac), [[51]](https://www.ey.com/en_gr/technical/tax/tax-alerts/regulation-eu-2023-2854-on-harmonised-rules-on-fair-access-to-and-use-of-data), but the Regulation stresses it does not create a GDPR legal basis for personal data sharing where the user is not the data subject [[39]](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/). EDPB guidelines show how connected vehicle data processing requires careful legal basis and minimization, and in eCall context mandates strict retention limits and transparency [[102]](https://www.edpb.europa.eu/system/files/2021-03/edpb_guidelines_202001_connected_vehicles_v2.0_adopted_en.pdf).

**Practical implication:** even when technology exists, **lawful access** for insurers/plaintiffs may require structured consent workflows, clear user controls, and privacy-preserving extraction.

---

## 7) Drawing the boundaries of responsibility: a structured allocation approach

A defensible liability allocation in Level 2 shared-control crashes should be built from **four questions**, each tied to technical facts and legal doctrines:

### Q1 — Was the system used within its declared (or reasonably implied) ODD?
- If clearly outside ODD (e.g., low traction, poor markings, visibility), driver negligence arguments strengthen.
- But if ODD is **not enforced** and the design permits easy engagement in unsafe contexts, “foreseeable misuse” and design defect arguments strengthen—especially where regulators criticize permissive operating capabilities [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf).

**Evidence:** OEM ODD statements/manuals; UI prompts; roadway/weather; system logs; recall materials [[122]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCLRPT-23V838-8276.PDF), [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf).

### Q2 — Did the driver meet the Level 2 duty of supervision and OEDR?
Per SAE Level 2 definition, the driver must supervise and perform OEDR [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf). Distraction, impairment, speeding, and ignoring warnings strongly support comparative negligence.

**Evidence:** EDR speed/brake, phone records, DMS/torque “nag” events, admissions.

### Q3 — Did the product reasonably mitigate foreseeable misuse and human limitations?
This is the design-defect heartland:
- Are driver engagement controls proportionate to system authority and ease-of-use?
- Are warnings salient and escalating?
- Is mode confusion likely?
- Are known limitations (false negatives, false positives) disclosed and mitigated?

ISO 21448 supports treating HMI confusion, attention loss, and foreseeable misuse as part of intended functionality safety [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf). NHTSA ODI has explicitly framed weak engagement controls as inappropriate for a permissive system and as leading to foreseeable misuse [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf).

### Q4 — Did marketing, naming, or representations induce unreasonable reliance?
Misleading advertising can shift responsibility because it changes what reliance is “reasonable.” But courts demand specific reliance proof (as in *Wang*) [[78]](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCOURTS-nyed-1_20-cv-03040/pdf/USCOURTS-nyed-1_20-cv-03040-1.pdf). Regulators have acted against autonomy-leaning branding/claims as misleading (California DMV documents; Munich decision as reported) [[133]](https://www.thedrive.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/08/21-02189-California-DMV-Tesla-Autopilot-Complaint.pdf), [[135]](https://dpadocs.dpaq.de/21831_12_16_25_Tesla_Administrative_Decision_Released_by_DMV.pdf), [[87]](https://www.cnbc.com/2020/07/14/tesla-autopilot-self-driving-false-advertising-germany.html).

---

## 8) Recommendations: regulatory guidelines for clearer, fairer liability allocation in Level 1–2 shared-control

These recommendations aim to (i) reduce crash frequency, (ii) reduce ambiguity in fault allocation, and (iii) align incentives so that responsibility follows control and knowledge.

### 8.1 Adopt legally-defined terminology and prohibit autonomy-leaning names for Level 2
**Guideline**
- For Level 2 systems, prohibit terms that imply the car “drives itself” (e.g., “self-driving,” “autopilot”) unless accompanied by standardized, prominent qualifiers and standardized capability statements.
- Require standardized disclosure: “Level 2—driver must supervise and perform OEDR at all times,” aligned with SAE’s Level 2 role allocation [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf) and NHTSA’s description that human must remain fully engaged [[8]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/laws-regulations/standing-general-order-crash-reporting).

**Rationale**
Marketing enforcement actions show naming/claims can mislead and increase misuse risk [[133]](https://www.thedrive.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/08/21-02189-California-DMV-Tesla-Autopilot-Complaint.pdf), [[87]](https://www.cnbc.com/2020/07/14/tesla-autopilot-self-driving-false-advertising-germany.html), [[135]](https://dpadocs.dpaq.de/21831_12_16_25_Tesla_Administrative_Decision_Released_by_DMV.pdf). SAE warns levels are often misinterpreted and that “2+” framing is misleading [[3]](https://users.ece.cmu.edu/~koopman/j3016/).

### 8.2 Require ODD declaration + machine-enforced ODD constraints (not just manuals)
**Guideline**
- Mandate that OEMs publish a **machine-readable ODD declaration** for each Level 2 feature (road type, speed range, weather/visibility constraints, lane marking requirements).
- Require the system to implement **ODD enforcement**: refuse activation or degrade to a safer assist mode with strong warnings when ODD confidence is low (e.g., glare, fog, missing lane lines).

**Rationale**
SOTIF identifies ODD exit and perception insufficiency as key hazard sources [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf). ODI’s Autopilot analysis criticized permissive capabilities combined with weak controls [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf).

### 8.3 Minimum driver monitoring standards proportional to control authority
**Guideline**
- For any system providing sustained lateral + longitudinal control, require **robust driver monitoring** (hands + attention), not only steering torque nags, with clearly specified performance metrics (time-to-detect inattention, false accept/reject tolerances).
- Require escalating warnings and **mandatory disengagement** after repeated noncompliance, similar in concept to emerging regulatory expectations around driver availability recognition and escalation [[5]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/29884732/ACSF-06-13%20%28J%29%20Proposal%20to%20amend%20R79%20based%20on%20ACSF-05-16.pdf).

**Rationale**
Human factors research shows partial automation increases off-road glances and secondary tasks [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33385962/), [[21]](https://escholarship.org/content/qt4j17h6xw/qt4j17h6xw.pdf). NTSB repeatedly found ineffective driver engagement monitoring contributes to complacency/inattention [[129]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/Pages/HWY18FH011.aspx), [[130]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/AccidentReports/Reports/HAR2001.pdf).

### 8.4 Standardize Level 2 event logging (“ADAS EDR”) and neutral access
**Guideline**
Create a standardized, privacy-preserving “ADAS EDR” dataset (in addition to crash delta-V data), including:
- ADAS engagement status and mode changes (per feature)
- reason for disengagement (driver override vs system limit)
- warning events (type, timing, duration)
- driver monitoring state (attentive/not attentive, hands detected)
- control inputs (steer/brake/accelerator) at higher sample rate near events
- for severe crashes, optional hashed references to sensor snapshots (not full video by default), with strict access controls

Require:
- defined triggers (airbag deploy, hard braking, collision, near-miss thresholds)
- minimum pre-event window (e.g., 30s) consistent with international DSSAD concepts and EDR expansion trends [[98]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/140711890/UNR157-12-08-Rev1.pdf), [[97]](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/12/18/2024-29862/event-data-recorders)
- **neutral retrieval** via standardized interface, with chain-of-custody integrity

**Rationale**
Traditional EDR often lacks ADAS state, while OEM proprietary logs can be decisive yet asymmetric [[97]](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/12/18/2024-29862/event-data-recorders), [[111]](https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/eng-2020-0007/pdf). UNECE ALKS introduces DSSAD precisely to determine driver-system interactions [[38]](https://unece.org/sites/default/files/2024-07/R157am4e_1.pdf).

### 8.5 Clarify post-sale software update duties and update-related defenses
**Guideline**
- Require OEMs to maintain a safety management process for ADAS software, including field monitoring, incident trend reporting, and timely mitigation—consistent with SOTIF’s lifecycle perspective [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf).
- Define when an update is “safety-critical,” how it is notified, and minimum friction for installation.
- Allocate responsibility where:
  - OEM fails to patch known safety defects within reasonable time → OEM share increases
  - user refuses safety-critical update after clear notice → user share increases (mirroring AEVA logic for update failures) [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18)

**Rationale**
UK AEVA explicitly contemplates liability implications for failure to install safety-critical updates [[57]](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2018/18). Revised EU PLD analyses emphasize software/updates and disclosure burdens [[37]](https://www.osborneclarke.com/insights/how-do-eu-product-liability-regulations-support-innovation-and-what-do-protect-against), [[44]](https://cms.law/en/fra/news-information/new-evidence-law-in-product-liability).

### 8.6 Create a “shared-control comparative responsibility schedule” for courts/insurers (soft law)
**Guideline**
Issue regulator/standards-body guidance that presumptively allocates fault ranges by scenario, adjustable by evidence:
- Driver distraction with clear warnings and proper DMS operation → driver majority share
- DMS ineffective/defeated too easily; permissive use outside safe road types → OEM share rises (consistent with ODI reasoning) [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf)
- Mode confusion caused by UI design or override behavior not clearly communicated → OEM share rises (ISO 21448: mode confusion hazard) [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf)
- Phantom braking or other ADAS-introduced hazards → OEM share rises (NHTSA recognizes unintended braking risk) [[13]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2024-11/NCAP-Final-Decision-Notice-Advanced-Driver-Assistance-Systems-Roadmap-11182024-web.pdf)

**Rationale**
This improves predictability, reduces litigation cost, and aligns incentives with controllability and knowledge.

### 8.7 Strengthen regulator access to exposure-normalized data (without misleading public metrics)
**Guideline**
Require OEMs to report:
- ADAS miles by mode/ODD category
- near-miss rates (hard braking, FCW, driver takeover)
- normalized crash involvement rates

**Rationale**
NHTSA cautions current incident reporting is not exposure-normalized and varies by telemetry capability, limiting interpretability [[9]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2022-06/ADAS-L2-SGO-Report-June-2022.pdf). Better denominators improve both safety oversight and fairness in defect adjudication.

---

## 9) Bottom line
In Level 2 shared-control crashes, **the driver is presumptively responsible for OEDR and supervision** under the SAE allocation model [[1]](https://wiki.unece.org/download/attachments/128418539/SAE%20J3016_202104.pdf), but **OEM responsibility expands** where design and marketing:
- create foreseeable overreliance (weak engagement controls with high authority) [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf), [[92]](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/accidentreports/reports/har1702.pdf)
- permit easy operation outside safe ODD without enforcement [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf)
- produce mode confusion or inadequate warnings (SOTIF/HMI failures) [[2]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/77490/d9843a45e11947e0aa79aaf2f00b65a8/ISO-21448-2022.pdf), [[15]](https://cdn.standards.iteh.ai/samples/69238/02ee6a4ef27f451f83c8b338c0dac275/ISO-15005-2017.pdf)
- introduce hazards like unintended braking that create new crash risks [[13]](https://www.nhtsa.gov/sites/nhtsa.gov/files/2024-11/NCAP-Final-Decision-Notice-Advanced-Driver-Assistance-Systems-Roadmap-11182024-web.pdf)

Recent regulatory actions (notably NHTSA’s Autosteer misuse recall and ODI findings) and litigation outcomes (e.g., Florida Keys verdict as reported) show a growing willingness to treat “misuse” as **foreseeable and design-relevant**, not merely driver moral failure—pushing liability toward a **shared responsibility** model grounded in control authority, engagement enforcement, and evidence transparency [[122]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/rcl/2023/RCLRPT-23V838-8276.PDF), [[125]](https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/inv/2022/INCLA-EA22002-14498.pdf), [[77]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c93dqpkwx4xo).
